sub: Build your own drone-software project — the BlueSky airspace competition is one way to take it on

title: Project Ideas 1–6: Build On the Drone
desc: Concrete builds on top of the DJI client (labs/dji-hello-world) — pick one, combine a few, or bring your own

rows: Live telemetry dashboard
Subscribe via KeyManager to position, altitude, attitude, battery, satellites and link status; render a real-time HUD on the phone
Flight-track logger & replay
Record a flight to a file (CSV / GeoJSON) and visualise the 3D track afterwards — feeds into the GIS module (13)
Camera stream & onboard vision
Pull the live video feed into the app, grab frames and run simple detection (marker, QR or colour blob) with an on-screen overlay
Autonomous pattern flyer
Fly a waypoint square, orbit or lawn-mower scan — first in the DJI simulator, then on real hardware with margin
Closed-loop precision movement
Replace the lab's open-loop timed hops with distance / position control driven by live telemetry feedback
Geofence & safety guard
Enforce a soft boundary from the live position; auto return-to-home or land when the drone nears the limit

title: Project Ideas 7–11: Backend & Data Pipeline
desc: Take the drone's data off the phone and into the course's technology stack

rows: Telemetry REST / streaming service
Push live telemetry to a Spring service described with OpenAPI; fan it out over SSE or WebSocket (Modules 7–8)
Message-queue telemetry pipeline
Publish tracks to a topic; independent consumers log, monitor and raise alerts in parallel (Module 9)
CQRS flight-state service
Separate the command side (mission commands) from the query side (current & historical state) for the drone (Module 10)
Readable API test suite in Karate
Contract tests for the telemetry / command backend that the whole team can read and run (Module 11)
Flight data store & query API
Persist flights and expose an API to search, compare and replay them

title: Project Ideas 12–16: Integration, Simulation & Ops
desc: Put the real drone and the simulator into the same air-traffic picture

rows: UTM monitor fed by a real drone
Feed the Module 6 UTM-style monitor with the Mini 4 Pro's live position — show it as a tracked aircraft
Real-time GIS map of live tracks
Plot one or many live drone positions on a moving map (Module 13)
BlueSky ↔ real-drone bridge
Inject the real drone as an aircraft into the BlueSky simulator, or run a sim scenario alongside a real flight
Multi-drone coordination & spacing
Simulate several tracks (sim plus one real) and detect & space conflicts between them
Collision avoidance in BlueSky
Study bluesky/plugins/asas, then build and measure your own resolver — the competition topic

title: The Competition: Collision Avoidance in BlueSky
desc: We put theory aside and let your code fight for survival

items: We split into groups — each group develops a collision-avoidance system for the airspace
All systems run in the BlueSky air traffic simulator (TU Delft, open source)
Scoring is brutally simple: the group that survives longest without a collision wins
Starting point: the bluesky/plugins/asas conflict-resolution plugins — study them, then beat them
Setup, scenarios and rules of the game: Module 12

title: Expectations: The Path to Grade 1.0
desc: The bar is deliberately high — this is a master's module, and working software you understand is its currency

rows: ≥ 60 hours of independent work
That is the entry ticket, not the goal — a 1.0 usually takes more. The effort must be traceable across the whole semester: repo history, notes, iterations
Explain — and modify — live
At the live demo expect "now change X" and follow-up questions that continue until it is clear the work is yours. Every design decision needs a reason you can defend
A working, practical build
Your own idea on top of the DJI client (Module 3), running on real hardware or the simulator — plus your own critical judgement of where the SDK's limits are
Engineering & measurement
An approach you designed, measured and can defend with data — e.g. if you enter the competition: why does your airspace survive, and at what traffic density does it stop?

items: A last-week sprint — your repo history will show it
Code that runs but that you cannot explain — it is graded as if it were missing
A demo that only works in one rehearsed run — or AI output you cannot explain line by line
Competition parameters tweaked until one demo run happened to pass
